posts.caption:
Around five years ago, New York-based Dutch photographer @pieterhenket was frustrated with the negativity he saw directed at young people. “I would hear from my older friends that the younger generation weren’t doing anything interesting,” he says. ⁠
⁠
Assembling a diverse group of queer young people cast from streets, parks, agencies, through connections and word of mouth, Henket recruited Mexico City-born stylist and Willy Chavarria collaborator @chino.castilla to help bring his nonconformist vision to life. ⁠
⁠
The result is a series of delineated black-and-white portraits with costuming that blends recognisable Mexican symbols – serapes, football kits, a Luchador mask – with operatic flare in the shape of horns, balloons, chaps, corsets and outsized silhouettes. ⁠
⁠
@ananda_pellerin speaks with Henket about the resulting photo book published by @damiani_books at the link in bio 📲⁠
⁠
📸 Birds of Mexico City by Pieter Henket. © Pieter Henket. Courtesy of and published by Damiani Books ⁠
1. La Mujer, from the series Birds of Mexico City, 2021⁠
2. El Angel, from the series Birds of Mexico City, 2021⁠
3. Primavera #5, from the series Birds of Mexico City, 2021⁠
5. La gallina, from the series Birds of Mexico City, 2021⁠
6. El Amante #2, from the series Birds of Mexico City, 2021⁠
⁠
⁠
⁠

posts.caption:
Since rising to prominence in the 1970s, photographer @sarahmoon_official has enchanted audiences with her ethereal work, which has a painterly and abstract texture that renders her images dreamlike and a little ghostly. ⁠
⁠
“Sarah believes strongly in the atmosphere of a photograph or picture, rather than simply documentation,” says her gallerist @michaelhoppengallery, who has worked with Moon for 37 years. Open now, the gallery presents a new exhibition of her work spanning 2003 to the present, the fifth solo show in a nearly four-decade collaboration. ⁠
⁠
“That is what drives my creative energy, knowing there is something ethereal, something different in the air,” Moon tells @laurahavlin. Read more at the link in bio 📲⁠
⁠
📸 Photography by #SarahMoon. Courtesy of Michael Hoppen Gallery⁠
1. Yue pour Yohji Yamamoto, 2019⁠
2. Oiseau des îles, 2003⁠
3. Andrea pour Yohji Yamamoto (VII), 2022⁠
5. Djenice for Yohji Yamamoto (II), 2022⁠

posts.caption:
One hundred years since her birth (on 1 June, 1926), Marilyn Monroe remains one of the most mythologised and photographed figures of the 20th century. Yet, of all the raked-over facets of her life, the idea of Marilyn Monroe as an avid reader is one crucial but less-explored aspect of her legacy. ⁠
⁠
Inspired by a list of Monroe’s library in her estate sale, @crowther_gail’s new book, Marilyn and Her Books, takes a look at the legendary actress’s life through the prism of the literature she consumed. “I have always been fascinated by other people’s bookshelves and what they can say about a person, so I immediately wanted to engage in an in-depth shelf-snoop of Marilyn’s library to see what that would uncover,” Crowther tells @ohnoitsemilyd. ⁠
⁠
Read more at the link in bio 📲⁠
⁠
📸 #MarilynMonroe reading Ulysses by James Joyce, Long Island, 1955 © Eve Arnold

posts.caption:
In 2002, Yohji Yamamoto did something unexpected, he stepped away from his all-black uniform and brought colour and graphics onto the Pour Homme runway.
The collection came from a collaboration with Japanese brush artist Saeko Tsuemura, whose retro Showa-era portraits were printed directly onto the garments.
But instead of just placing images on fabric, Yohji built the clothes around them, so the faces and compositions wouldn’t get broken by seams or construction.
It completely shifted the mood, taking familiar references like pin-up culture and flipping them through a Japanese post-war lens, in Yohji’s own way.
The show also quietly pointed toward the future of luxury sportswear, with early Adidas collaborations that would later evolve into Y-3.
posts.alt_text: Photo by Archived Runway on May 20, 2026. May be a graphic of magazine, poster and text that says '2이일 ARCHIVED ARCHIVEDRUNWAY RUNWAY The Time Yohji Swapped His All- All-Black Code For Graphic Prints'.

posts.caption:
The CHANEL Cruise 2026/27 collection, ‘Sous le salon la plage’ shot by @christinecentenera
This marks The Cruise debut of creative director Matthieu Blazy. Held at the Casino Municipal in Biarritz, France, the show honored the coastal town where Gabrielle Chanel opened her first couture house in 1915.
The designs bridged rigid salon elegance with relaxed shoreline ease .Blazy opened the runway by reinterpreting Chanel’s iconic 1926 little black dress, cleverly transforming its back-bow into a functional clutch.
Aquatic motifs heavily defined the collection, showcasing shimmering mermaid silhouettes, fish-scale paillettes, and open-weave fisherman-net knits.
The house’s signature heavy tweeds were softened into light, fluid separates, styled alongside striped Basque beach knits.
posts.alt_text: Photo shared by Archived Runway on May 20, 2026 tagging @christinecentenera. May be an image of grocery bag and text.

Congrats to Arsenal, the 2025/26 Premier League champions!
On 19 May, @arsenal won the Premier League title for the first time since 2004. After the match between Manchester City and Bournemouth, it ended in a draw (1–1), which meant Manchester City could not get the winning result. Consequently, this confirmed that Arsenal became the 2025/26 Premier League champions. To celebrate, a parade will take place in their home borough of Islington at 2 pm on 31 May.
Meanwhile, Arsenal are set to face PSG in the UEFA final on 30 May. If they win this match as well, it will be a historic moment. Swipe right to see the unforgettable moments!⚽️🏆

posts.caption:
Did Isak Andic, the founder of Mango, fall to his death — or was he pushed by his son?
In December 2024, Jonathan Andic was hiking alone with his father in the Montserrat mountains near Barcelona when Isak Andic fell more than 300 feet to his death. The incident was initially ruled a tragic accident, with police corroborating Jonathan’s account at the time.
But more than a year later, Jonathan Andic was arrested on suspicion of homicide after investigators reportedly uncovered inconsistencies in his testimony. Spanish media also described tensions within the billionaire family, including reported disputes over inheritance and Jonathan’s role within the company. A family spokesperson has denied any wrongdoing, stating that Jonathan is innocent and that the family’s “cooperation has been and will remain total.”
At the time of his death, Isak Andic was worth an estimated $4 billion. Born in Istanbul in 1953, he built Mango from a single shop in Barcelona into one of the world’s largest fashion retailers, with nearly 3,000 stores globally and over $4.3 billion in annual revenue.
Following his father’s death, Jonathan Andic became chairman of the family holding company alongside his sisters Judith and Sarah, inheriting control of the fashion empire.
Words by Josephine Jiang
Next-Level Fashion, Daily.
Follow @a2z_fashionmag Now.
📷 Miquel Benitez, Àlex Garcia, @mango
#mango #isakandic

posts.caption:
Over her stellar 20-year career, @phoebejtonkin has captivated audiences. The Sydney-born, New York-based actor began acting as a teenager playing a mermaid in the series ‘H2O: Just Add Water,’ and has since achieved a dynamic array of dramatic parts, a stint in the director’s chair and a new role producing and starring in the adaptation of crime thriller, ‘The Dark Lake’. While she’s relishing the collaborative process, the actor and May 2026 cover star tells BAZAAR, acting remains her first love.“I feel really lucky to be invited into the collaborative process so early on in a project. Usually, as an actor, you turn up and you’re told, ‘This is what your set looks like, these are your fellow actors’. And with this, I get to be there from the inception of the project and be part of these decisions.”
Through the link in bio, Phoebe Tonkin reflects on her career and attraction to robust roles that “lean into the complexities of being a woman.”
Phoebe Tonkin wears @tiffanyandco HardWear collection for the May 2026 issue of Harper’s BAZAAR Australia/New Zealand.

posts.caption:
“I’m attracted to things that are authentic and grounded,” @phoebejtonkin tells BAZAAR of her latest role in the cross-genre ‘Two Years Later’, a post-pandemic romantic dramedy set in Brisbane. Tonkin plays Emily, a woman with a complicated love and family life who sets out on a series of dates with Ryan, played by Brenton Thwaites, to work out if they’re destined to marry. “There was something quite relatable and ordinary about these two people who are in their 30s and trying to navigate dating, coming out of a pandemic, all the isolation and loneliness that they’ve been dealing with. And I just thought it was really real,” she shares. It was this funny, quirky love story drama that you don’t get to do often.”
Continue reading Phoebe Tonkin’s cover story through the link in bio.

posts.caption:
In an industry where surnames are inherited currencies, Bazaar India’s April-May cover star, Tabu (@tabutiful), has operated for over three decades on a mononym. To understand her career is to understand what it meant to say yes to things the industry was not ready for and to have the instinct to recognise those things as worthy, even when no template existed for them. But she refuses to call it rebellion. “It was simply a reflection of who I am. The roles I chose, the decisions I made—they came from some similar place throughout my life. Some part of me has been completely unchanged,” she insists.
Read the full cover story at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
#BazaarOnStands: Rasika Dugal (@rasikadugal) has built a body of work that lives beyond the glamour of screens, rooted instead in craft, restraint, and emotional precision. From the moral intensity of ‘Delhi Crime’, to the smouldering quiet of Beena Tripathi in ‘Mirzapur’, and the layered vulnerability of ‘Out of Love’, her performances linger for their honesty rather than volume. It is this deep respect for storytelling—and a curiosity for the unfamiliar—that shapes her choices, keeping her deliberately selective as she makes space for more women-led narratives that continue to evolve, still very much in the making.
Editor: Rasna Bhasin (@rasnabhasin)
Interview: Jhanvi Duggal (@jhanvii.23)

posts.caption:
When Swiss sneaker brand On reported its latest results last week, the company couldn’t help boasting about its profitability.
On hit a new record for the first quarter of the year. Gross margin — a measure of how much profit a company makes on sales of its goods after subtracting production costs — reached 64.2 percent, up from 59.9 percent the year before.
That figure far outpaces the margins of larger, established rivals such as Nike and Adidas, and sits closer to top luxury companies like Hermés at 71.1 percent.
Read the full analysis from BoF's Mike Sykes (@mikedsykes) to learn how On has managed to maintain such high margins, and why some are sceptical that the brand will be able to sustain them forever. #linkinbio
posts.alt_text: Photo by The Business of Fashion on May 20, 2026. posts.caption:
Chanel sales returned to growth last year, shaking off a punishing industry downturn with investments in new stores and a sweeping creative revamp that positions the French couture and beauty giant to gain further ground on rivals in the coming months.
New designs by creative director Matthieu Blazy are still trickling into stores but the designer’s runway debut in October, followed by buzzy outings from New York to Biarritz, have already generated renewed excitement for the label.
Sales began to grow by high single-digit percentage points in the second half of the year across product categories and regions, momentum that has continued through the first few months of this year, CFO Philippe Blondiaux said.
Read the full story at the #linkinbio
✍️ @mimosaspencer
📷 @chanelofficial
posts.alt_text: Photo by The Business of Fashion on May 19, 2026. May be an image of standing, suit, magazine, poster, footwear, newspaper and text that says 'BOF BREAKING NEWS Chanel Returns to Growth as Blazymania Kicks In'.

posts.caption:
Polyester has become widely known as an inexpensive material used by fast fashion, yet design-forward labels have also relied on it for decades, not because it was cheap but because of what it allowed them to create: sculptural silhouettes and material structures impossible to achieve in quite the same way with many natural fibres.
BoF's Shayeza Walid (@shayeza_) explores why surging polyester prices aren’t deterring the high-end brands that rely on the fabric for its versatility. Read more #linkinbio
📷 @isseymiyakeofficial, @gauravguptaofficial, @cfcl_official
posts.alt_text: Photo by The Business of Fashion on May 19, 2026. May be an image of magazine, shawl and text that says 'BOF Why High Fashion Can't Quit Polyester The material may be most closely associated with fast fashion, but high-end labels depend on it heavily because of its versatility. They're not giving it up, even as surging fossil fuel prices push up costs.'.

posts.caption:
Somewhere between a Go-Pro and a hidden camera, Meta glasses have introduced a new genre of social media videos that allows curious consumers to peer into the exclusive world of ultra-luxury shopping.
But the rise of POV retail videos present a challenge for luxury labels and their carefully controlled brands. The videos often generate negative sentiment, in particular when they feature customer service experiences that can be perceived as elitist and quickly turn into a public relations liability.
BoF’s Austin Kim (@austintaekim) explores how luxury brands are adapting to the era of discreet, always-on content creation. Read more #linkinbio
📷 @raybanmeta
posts.alt_text: Photo by The Business of Fashion on May 18, 2026. May be an image of magazine, sunglasses and text that says 'BOF Rybu P Why ρον Shopping Videos Are a Risk for Luxury Point-of-view videos secretly filmed inside luxury boutiques by content creators wearing smartglasses are going viral on TikTok and Instagram, creating reputational risk for luxury brands from Gucci to Patek Philippe.'.

posts.caption:
After queuing for days to get their hands on the new Audemars Piguet-Swatch collaboration, fans on Saturday finally got their chance to buy the eye-catching pop-art timepiece, but heaving crowds stopped some stores from opening their doors and prompted pleas for calm.
Such was the frenzy for the timepiece at the Dubai Mall and the Mall of the Emirates in Dubai that the launch was canceled over crowd safety concerns, Swatch said on its Instagram account. Similar scenes played out in the UK, France, US and Singapore.
“To ensure the safety of both our customers and our staff in Swatch stores, we kindly ask you not to rush to our stores in large numbers to acquire this product,” the company said in a statement on its Instagram account, adding that the new timepieces will be available for several months.
Read the full story #linkinbio
✍️ Allegra Catelli (Bloomberg)
📷 @AudemarsPiguet, @Swatch

posts.caption:
For the global luxury industry, the last two years have been defined by a prolonged period of meagre growth, macro-uncertainty, and a slow recovery in the critical Chinese market. But as we move further into 2026, the strategic imperative has shifted. It is no longer enough to simply wait for the cycle to turn; leadership now requires navigating a rapidly-changing environment where geopolitical volatility and technological disruption have become the baseline.
In this episode of The BoF Podcast, Jonathan Wingfield (@misterwingfield), editor-in-chief of System Magazine joins Imran Amed (@imranamed) and Luca Solca, managing director and global head of luxury goods at Bernstein, for their regular seasonal conversation on the state of the industry. They analyse this new industry paradigm through two distinct lenses: the clinical, data-driven reality of the equity markets, and the visceral, creative pulse of culture. They examining the collapse of the old narrative within luxury, why brand heat has become a lazy currency, and why the real threat of AI isn’t the technology itself, but the professionals who master it first.
🎙️ Listen now #linkinbio #TheBoFPodcast
📷 System Collections Issue No. 3. Photograph by Johnny Dufort. Styling by Suzanne Koller.

posts.caption:
On Monday evening, it took auction house @christiesinc just 40 minutes to sell more than $630 million in art, smashing records for both the painter Jackson Pollock and sculptor Constantin Brancusi. Later in the night, it rounded things off with yet more broken records and another $490 million.
The first part of the $1.1-billion New York sale featured 16 works from S.I. Newhouse's collection. It marked the fourth time Christie's has brought a group of works to market from the late media giant, who owned @condenast, as well as large slice of America's local newspapers and broadcast stations.
But it was the first time that the auction house tapped @nicolekidman for a promotional video, filming her free-flowing encounter with the golden Brancusi bust "Danaïde," which was poised to be one of the night's star lots. The video was based on a 1930s Man Ray film of fellow surrealist Lee Miller, per Christie's, and while it didn't quite take off in the same way as Kidman's viral AMC ad, it did once again prove her commitment to any and all roles.
Monday's sales at Christie's also included three masterworks from the collection of former Museum of Modern Art president Agnes Gund, which set a new $98.4 million record for Mark Rothko, while Sotheby's offered up treasures worth $166.3 million from the late art dealer Robert Mnuchin last week.
📸 : Hunter Abrams/Courtesy Christie's
posts.alt_text: Photo shared by CNN Style on May 19, 2026 tagging @christiesinc, and @nicolekidman. May be a graphic of one or more people, people standing, poster, magazine and text that says 'CHRISTIE'S SELLS $1.1 BILLION IN ART IN ONE NIGHT - WITH A LITTLE HELP FROM NICOLE KIDMAN CNN'.

posts.caption:
On Sunday, @swatch issued a social media statement urging customers "not to rush to our stores in large numbers," citing concern for the safety of staff and shoppers after the launch of "Royal Pop," a series of pocket watches modeled on @audemarspiguet's iconic Royal Oak.
It added: "In some countries, queues of more than 50 people cannot be accepted, and sales may need to be paused." On its website, Swatch meanwhile assured customers that the collection will "remain available for several months."
Swatch's policy of limiting purchase to one per customer did little to deter scalpers. The items immediately flooded resale sites over the weekend, with prices on watch marketplace Chrono24 ranging from around $1,200 to almost $6,000 – a sharp increase of the original $400 – $420 price tag.
Videos shared online showed fights breaking out and security guards struggling to contain large crowds. A number of stores around the world closed due to safety concerns.
Like other "high-low" collaborations — from @stellamccartney working with @hm to @jw_anderson's @uniqlo collections — the pitch to customers is clear: A chance to own an exclusive brand that might otherwise be out of reach.
AP's former CEO François-Henri Bennahmias, once previously praised a similar collaboration between Omega x Swatch, which saw long lines forming outside Swatch boutiques in 2022.
A proud Swatch fan, Bennahmias said in an interview with Luxury Tribune that the Omega collaboration was a "great idea" that did not "affect the integrity of Omega at all … because it educates the younger generation about the icons of watchmaking."
Whether fights, mall frenzies and arrests change the calculation for AP is another matter altogether.
📸 : Swatch/Matthew Horwood/Getty Images
posts.alt_text: Photo shared by CNN Style on May 18, 2026 tagging @swatch, and @audemarspiguet. May be an image of wrist watch and text that says 'UIEIP ಶಿ SWATCH AND AUDEMARS PIGUET'S 'HIGH-LOW' 'HIGH COLLAB SPARKED A FRENZY. WHO'S THE REAL WINNER?'.

posts.caption:
The long tailored coat has always belonged to people who understand presence.
Before it became a fashion statement, it was a garment of function: protection, structure, authority. Military greatcoats, officer coats, riding coats, city overcoats, all of them were built around the same idea: length creates command. A short jacket moves with the body. A long coat changes the body.
That is the power of tailoring. It doesn’t simply cover; it edits. The shoulder is sharpened, the torso is lengthened, the walk becomes slower, more deliberate. A well-cut coat gives the wearer architecture before they even enter a room.
In fashion, the long coat has moved through many lives. It has been masculine, cinematic, severe, romantic, corporate, rebellious. On women, especially, it carries a particular tension: the language of menswear, reworked into something colder, softer, more dangerous. The best versions never feel decorative. They feel controlled.
What makes a long tailored coat good is not only the fabric, but the discipline behind it. The weight of the wool, the fall of the lapel, the placement of the button, the line from shoulder to hem. Too little structure and it becomes a robe. Too much and it becomes costume. The perfect coat sits somewhere in between: precise, but not stiff; dramatic, but not theatrical.
Today, its appeal feels even sharper. In a fashion landscape obsessed with novelty, the long tailored coat offers something rarer: permanence. It doesn’t rely on trend. It relies on proportion, fabric, and attitude.
A long coat does not complete an outfit.
It becomes the outfit.
posts.alt_text: Photo by CULT MAGAZINEⓇ on May 19, 2026. May be an image of duffle coat, overcoat, suit and text that says 'CULT selects: Long Tailored Coats. CULT'.

posts.caption:
Elizaveta Porodina approaches fashion photography less like a traditional editorial and more like a piece of visual theatre. In Carnival for Numéro Netherlands, fashion becomes surreal, emotional and slightly unsettling at the same time. The images move between fantasy and distortion, using exaggerated beauty looks, mirrored faces, sculptural silhouettes and dramatic lighting to create an atmosphere that feels closer to performance art than commercial fashion photography.
What makes Porodina’s work stand out is how immersive it feels. Every image looks completely constructed, yet never cold or overly digital. Her use of color, shadows and texture gives the photographs a painterly quality, almost like contemporary portraits inspired by cinema, surrealism and classical art all at once. Even the strangest details still feel human.
Maggie Maurer carries the editorial almost like a character moving through different emotional states rather than simply modeling clothes. One frame feels soft and ghostlike, the next intense or confrontational. The beauty work by Cécile Paravina plays a huge role in shaping that transformation. Bleached brows, graphic textures, metallic skin and distorted facial details push the makeup beyond beauty and into image making itself.
What makes Carnival so memorable is that it fully commits to its own visual language. It doesn’t try to feel wearable or commercially safe. Instead, it reminds people that fashion editorials can still be strange, emotional and imaginative without losing their impact.
Editorial Credits
Editorial: Carnival
Publication: Numéro Netherlands Issue 02
Photography: Elizaveta Porodina
Model: Maggie Maurer
Styling: Lisa Jarvis
Makeup: Cécile Paravina
Hair: Olivier Schawalder
posts.alt_text: Photo by CULT MAGAZINEⓇ on May 18, 2026. May be a graphic of one or more people, makeup, hair, magazine and text that says 'The industry needs more editorials like this. CULT®'.

posts.caption:
Long before beauty tutorials and clean girl aesthetics, makeup was ritual, rebellion, theatre, status, protest. Ancient Egyptians painted their eyes almost like armor. In the 1920s, women used makeup as a symbol of freedom. And in fashion, the most interesting looks always came from people willing to look a little uncomfortable.
That’s why avant-garde makeup matters. The faceless skin, metallic bodies, distorted brows or surreal shapes aren’t supposed to feel safe, they’re supposed to make you stop and look. Fashion has always needed exaggeration, experimentation and a bit of madness to move forward. Otherwise everything starts looking the same.
Sometimes the best makeup look is the one that makes people ask: “wait… why does this make me feel weird?”
If you are featured in any of these images or are the makeup artist/creative behind one of the looks and would like to be credited, feel free to message us anytime so we can add the proper credits.
posts.alt_text: Photo by CULT MAGAZINEⓇ on May 17, 2026. May be a meme of one or more people, makeup, bust and text that says 'Normal makeup scares me. CULT®'.

posts.caption:
Feathers have always existed somewhere between fashion and fantasy. Long before modern couture, they were symbols of power, status and spectacle, worn in royal courts, ceremonial dress and later transformed into pure glamour by Hollywood and haute couture. By the 1920s, feathers had already become one of fashion’s most dramatic signatures, moving with the body in a way no other material really can.
What makes feathers so timeless is how easily they shift moods. They can feel elegant, dangerous, theatrical or completely surreal depending on the designer, the styling or the photograph itself. That’s probably why fashion photography keeps returning to them. Feathers don’t just add texture, they create movement, shadow and emotion. They make clothes feel alive.
Looking at these images, you can see how different that language can become. Some looks feel sharp and almost intimidating, especially the darker silhouettes and oversized black feathers that turn the models into these sculptural, unreal characters. Others feel softer and exaggerated in a more playful way, like the pale feathered dresses that almost float against the white background. Even the bright red look feels less like clothing and more like performance.
Fashion constantly reinvents feathers, but the reason they survive never changes: they make people look twice.
posts.alt_text: Photo by CULT MAGAZINEⓇ on May 16, 2026. May be an image of text that says 'Say what you want, Feathers matter. CULT'.

posts.caption:
Szilveszter Makó is one of the few fashion photographers right now whose images feel instantly recognizable. The Hungarian born, Milan based photographer creates editorials that look less like traditional fashion shoots and more like fragments from a strange theatre play, an old folk tale, or a forgotten painting hanging in a museum somewhere. His work sits in this unusual space between fashion, performance art and set design, where every object feels handmade and every pose feels carefully rehearsed.
What makes Makó’s images stand out is how physical and constructed they feel. In an era where so much fashion photography is overly polished and digitally perfected, his work feels tactile and human. You can see the painted textures, handmade props and imperfect edges of the sets. Nothing feels sterile. Even the most surreal images still carry a certain warmth and intimacy.
A lot of his editorials feel like stage productions frozen into a single frame. Models are boxed into geometric spaces, surrounded by strange objects, dressed more like characters than fashion subjects. There are references to theatre, folklore, silent film and classical portraiture, but it never feels nostalgic in an obvious way. He uses those influences to build entirely new worlds around the clothes.
Light also plays a huge role in his work. Makó often avoids flash, which gives the photographs a softer, moodier atmosphere. His images feel cinematic without trying too hard. There is always tension inside them, even in the stillness.
What makes his photography so interesting from a fashion perspective is that the clothing never feels separate from the image itself. The garments become part of the story, part of the set, sometimes even part of the illusion. In a moment where so much fashion imagery starts looking the same, Makó’s work reminds people that editorials can still feel imaginative, theatrical and genuinely personal.
posts.alt_text: Photo by CULT MAGAZINEⓇ on May 15, 2026. May be a graphic of poster, magazine and text that says 'This Photographer made Fashion weird again. CULT'.

posts.caption:
For decades, diamond retail success was measured by one thing: volume.
More inventory.
More promotions.
More units sold.
That model is being rewritten.
The US, which accounts for 53% of global diamond jewellery demand — is shifting toward a value-driven retail model where consumers are buying fewer pieces but spending more per purchase.
Here’s what’s changing:
💎 Finished jewellery is outperforming loose diamonds
📈 Average ticket sizes are rising sharply
🔬 Lab-grown diamonds are dominating entry-level demand
✨ Natural diamonds are being repositioned as premium luxury
🛍 Storytelling, traceability and trust are driving conversions
The message for manufacturers, wholesalers and retailers is clear:
Success in 2026 won’t come from selling more.
It will come from selling smarter.
If your product offers stronger design, emotional significance and a compelling story, consumers are willing to pay for it.
What’s your biggest takeaway from this shift in US diamond retail?
#DiamondWorld #DiamondRetail #NaturalDiamonds #DWMagazine

posts.caption:
On May 19th, Jonathan Andic was arrested in connection to the death of his father Isak Andic, the billionaire founder of Mango, who died in 2024 after slipping into a ravine on a Montserrat hiking trail. Authorities have outlined new evidence against Jonathan, prompting his arrest as a homicide suspect.
Jonathan, the only witness to his father’s death, claimed that he was a few meters ahead on the hiking trail when his father paused to take photos of the scenery. He told emergency services that he heard the fall, but did not see it occur. He also claimed that his relationship with his father was good, despite known tensions around Jonathan’s business failings at Mango in 2015.
However, new evidence shows that Isak’s cell phone was found in his pocket at the time of his death, and no photos appear on his camera roll of the hike. As well, the autopsy report found no scuffs on Isak’s hands, suggesting that he fell backward, not forward, into the ravine. Examination of Isak’s shoes also showed that the soles were worn down “deliberately” to create the appearance of a slip. Jonathan was also found to have visited the hiking trail multiple times shortly before his father’s death, causing investigators to suspect premeditation.
Most importantly, Jonathan’s tense relationship with his father was illuminated by Estefanía Knuth, the professional golfer and long-term girlfriend of Isak (who happens to be in a legal dispute with Isak’s children for €70m of the inheritance.) Knuth’s claims are supported by WhatsApp messages between Jonathan and Isak which reveal the son’s “obsession with money” and his demands for a living inheritance, which Isak was “forced” to agree to.
Finally, in 2024, Jonathan learned that Isak had planned to set up a “foundation to help people in need.” WhatsApp messages show a shift in the son’s attitude towards his father, causing him to express a desire to reconcile their differences. Thus, Jonathan invited Isak on the hike in Montserrat, just the two of them, to “talk alone.” Wanting to recover his relationship with his son, Isak accepted the invitation that would lead to his untimely death, and the foundation was never realized.

posts.caption:
As AI looms over job prospects, it’s no surprise it has become an unwelcome topic at this year’s college commencements… well, at least for new grads anyway.
Two viral videos emerged over the past week showing graduation speakers gloating about the future of AI. Gloria Caulfield, a real estate exec, went viral after she looked visibly shocked as students booed her May 8 commencement speech at the University of Central Florida after comparing AI to the industrial revolution. “What happened?,” she asked. “I struck a nerve.”
That same energy popped up again at the University of Arizona, where former Google CEO Eric Schmidt delivered a similar message last Friday, but even more aggressively. “If you don’t care about science that’s okay,” he stated, “because AI is gonna touch everything else as well.” Schmidt reportedly anticipated backlash from students following allegations of rape and sexual harassment, and surveillance, filed in a lawsuit last November.
Online, the comments are basically echoing the same vibe. “Aren’t commencement addresses supposed to be motivating?,” one Threads user wrote. “Why do they keep sending AI simps to college graduations to brag to them about how AI is going to steal their jobs?,” another asked.
At Glendale Community College, dozens of graduate names were reportedly skipped and/or mispronounced by an AI reader replacing traditional announcers.
College president Dr. Tiffany Hernandez laughed off the tech issue, saying, “Each of you were able to walk the stage and get a picture, which is what I would hope is the most meaningful,” she said. “I am so sorry. There’s plenty of opportunities, I hope, to take some really good pictures and to celebrate you with your loved ones as well.”
Maybe AI just isn’t needed in a tradition that’s been doing fine for hundreds of years without it.
#graduation #2026 #jobs #news #reels

Diet PRADA'.

posts.caption:
Everlane has officially sold to Shein, the fast-fashion giant, in light of the company’s $90m debt burden.
The capsule-wardrobe retailer was founded in 2011, promising sustainability and “radical transparency” in their pricing and supply chain. Their target customer was a coming-of-age millennial who was eager for affordable fashion that aligned with their value systems. For years, Everlane remained the de facto workplace-casual retailer for that very customer, offering simple, non-branded, trend resistant options which supposedly didn’t come from sweatshops and took a bullheaded approach to cutting carbon emissions.
You could say that Everlane and brands like it defined the 2010s, to some extent. Everlane was a leader in a decade of optimistic retailers, each of which sold its own tailored promise of creating a better world through DTC sales; Reformation sold ethical occasion dresses, All Birds sold sneakers made of recycled materials, Outdoor Voices sold inclusive athleisure, and so on. The millennial utopia was, it seems, an ethical value proposition that consumers and investors could get behind.
But now, many of these brands are falling apart under the crushing truth of capitalism: Ethical consumption may not be as realistic on the large-scale as we’d hoped it could be. Everlane is, afterall, being acquired by the very force of retail that it was supposed to replace, one that would sooner churn out desirable styles and exploit consumers’ growing interest in hyper-trends and luxury than worry over the environment or supply chain problems like slave labour.
What does that mean for customers who relied on Everlane to provide a cohesive wardrobe sans effort, anxiety, or guilt? One Reddit post asks: “What are we buying/wearing to replace Everlane?” The responses are bleak, offering little in terms of replacements and much in terms of disappointment.
“I’m just so sick of everything. I just want quality clothing that’s not made from child labor and has ethical/sustainable production lines,” one user said.
Can a brand like that exist anymore? Or is the dream of the 2010s dead?
posts.alt_text: Photo by Diet Prada ™ on May 18, 2026. posts.caption:
#ELLECoverStar: There is a version of Zinnia Kumar (now Rose) (@zinniakumar) that fashion understands easily. The model. An image that moves seamlessly between Paris, New York, and wherever the industry gathers next. It is also the least interesting version of her. We’re at Le Bristol in Paris and the day carries all the familiar markers of a luxury cover shoot. Controlled light. Precise framing. Cartier anchoring the narrative. But Rose doesn’t perform that script in the expected way. She arrives ready, slips into each look without negotiation, barely checks the monitor. There is no sense of overworking the image, only an instinctive understanding of how to move within it. Head to the 🔗 in bio to read our conversation with the cover star.
On @zinniakumar: Clash de Cartier earrings in rose gold and green dyed agate, Clash de Cartier necklace in rose gold, Clash de Cartier ring in rose gold and chalcedony, Clash De Cartier ring in rose gold and onyx, and Clash de Cartier ring in rose gold and red dyed agate, all by @cartier. posts.caption:
#ELLECoverStar: Between takes, Zinnia Rose (@zinniakumar) speaks about working with domestic violence and coercive control survivors, particularly within South Asian communities. About helping women reclaim confidence, voice, and agency within legal systems that often fail to recognise the complexity of their experiences. “I haven’t spoken about this publicly before, but I spent ten years in an abusive, financially and coercively controlling marriage,” she shares. “It shaped how much of myself I could express. Reclaiming myself and healing has been a long process, and one of the first things I did was remove the name he gave me. I’m returning to my birthright, as Zinnia Jothi Rose.” Head to the 🔗 in bio to read our conversation with the cover star.
On @zinniakumar: Clash de Cartier earrings in rose gold and onyx, Clash de Cartier necklace in yellow gold and onyx, Clash de Cartier ring in yellow gold and onyx, and Clash de Cartier bracelet in yellow gold and onyx, all by @cartier. posts.caption:
#ELLECoverStar: As a South Asian woman in global fashion, visibility has never been a straightforward gain. It comes layered with projection, expectation, and the pressure to represent more than oneself. “Belonging begins internally,” Zinnia Rose (@zinniakumar) says. “Once that is anchored, the room stops being a mirror for old pain.” India has always been part of the global conversation. What has shifted is not presence, but recognition. Her own relationship to India reflects that complexity. Shaped by diaspora, by fragments rather than fixed geography, it resists easy definition. Home, for her, is not a place. It is something she carries. An emotional inheritance. Head to the 🔗 in bio to read our conversation with the cover star.
On @zinniakumar: Clash de Cartier earrings in rose gold and chalcedony, Clash de Cartier earrings in rose gold and green dyed agate, Clash de Cartier ring in rose gold and green dyed agate, and Clash de Cartier ring in rose gold and chalcedony, all by @cartier. posts.caption:
#ELLECoverStar: For someone whose profession is built on image, there is little interest in maintaining one off set. Zinnia Rose (@zinniakumar) speaks directly, answers without deflection, and is comfortable holding contradiction without resolving it. By the end of the day, we’ve wrapped up the images. The narrative, in many ways, goes on. Because what Zinnia Rose represents does not sit neatly within a single frame. She offers something more layered. Less immediate. Far more reflective of where fashion is trying, and often failing, to go.
Head to the 🔗 in bio to read our conversation with the cover star.

posts.caption:
#ELLECoverStar: Zinnia Rose’s (@zinniakumar) understanding of sustainability comes from lineage. From women who lived in close relationships with care, survival, and community. “My great-grandmother was a dhai, a traditional midwife. She sustained life in the most literal way,” she says. Five generations removed from India, that inheritance has not been diluted. If anything, it has sharpened. Sustainability, in her world, is not environmental alone. It is emotional. Cultural. Psychological. It is about what survives, what is carried forward, and what is protected when systems fail. “Sustainability, to me, is the long-term preservation of dignity, clarity, and emotional wellbeing,” she says. “Nothing endures if people don’t.” Head to the 🔗 in bio to read our conversation with the cover star.
On @zinniakumar:
Look 1: Clash de Cartier earrings in rose gold and onyx, Clash de Cartier necklace in rose gold and onyx, Clash de Cartier necklace in rose gold and red dyed agate, Clash de Cartier ring in rose gold and chalcedony, Clash de Cartier ring in rose gold and onyx, all by @cartier. posts.caption:
Lights, camera, @billieeilish.
At just 24 years old, the 10-time Grammy winner and two-time Oscar winner has existed in the public consciousness for a decade now. But this May, a new concert documentary attempts to capture her star power and stage presence in a new light—through the eyes of legendary director James Cameron, no less. Eilish never thought she’d make another documentary after 2021’s ‘Billie Eilish: The World’s a Little Blurry,’ which closely documented her adolescence and the making of her sensational 2019 debut album. “I’m so private about my actual life now,” Eilish tells ELLE, but when the ‘Avatar’ filmmaker came calling, she knew she had to pick up—and co-direct the film alongside him.
In ELLE’s May 2026 cover story, Eilish opens up about collaborating with Cameron, how her personal and professional relationship with her brother @finneas has evolved, and her activist spirit: “I make art and music to reach people, to help people.” Read the full interview at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
Summer should feel easy—or so says @haileybieber. After all, she's long been regarded as a savant of minimalist style, and when it comes to getting dressed for summer, the secret is having no strategy at all.
“For summer getaways specifically, I like to keep things as effortless as possible,” the model tells ELLE.
That essence is behind Bieber’s latest appearance in @calzedonia’s new summer campaign, coinciding with her new role as the Italian label’s global digital ambassador. Just for the occasion, she opens up to us about the new images, swimwear styling tips, and what she's looking forward to this summer.
Comment “summer” and you’ll receive a direct link in your DMs to shop now.

posts.caption:
Beauty lovers, rejoice: @marcjacobsbeauty is officially back!
When the beauty line first launched in 2013, its bold colors and experimental spirit made its products user favorites. So naturally, fans were disappointed when the line abruptly shuttered in 2021. (As was @themarcjacobs himself, who says: “Well, I did never want it to end. I was disappointed and sad to see it go.”)
Luckily for everyone, it’s back, and available at Sephora in June. For inspiration, the product team researched what fans loved from previous collections, putting together a seven-piece relaunch that seems to be just as inspired by joy mixed with gritty New York glam. Former faves (like the bestselling eyeliner formula) return with reimagined twists, and while none of the shades will be an exact match for previous ones, they will come close enough to spark nostalgia. “Listen, if this is what Marc Jacobs represents, then you need to put your money where your mouth is,” Jacobs tells ELLE.
Read the full interview—and get a closer look at the new products—at the link in bio.

posts.caption: Benito at the beach—with @zara, no less. After the two collaborated on his looks for both his Super Bowl LX halftime show and the 2026 Met Gala, the global superstar and brand have partnered to release a 150-piece collection, available starting May 21. Inspired by his signature personal style, “Benito Antonio” is functional and wearable, with oversize hoodies, mid-thigh track shorts, and distressed ringer tees grounding the collection. Complete with a photoshoot in his native Puerto Rico, the collection is already island-ready. Tap the link in bio for more details on the new collaboration.

Years into a formidable career, Charan isn’t chasing expectation… he’s refining it. Choosing presence over performance and meaning over mere spectacle.
We celebrate his journey with our latest cover, where the actor steps into a version of himself like never seen before.

posts.caption:
What an incredible finish to the 2025-26 Premier League race (@premierleague). Arsenal (@arsenal) were crowned champions of England for the first time in 22 years after Manchester City's (@manutd) draw at Bournemouth on Tuesday night.
In a topsy turvy season, where Arsenal had the title in their sights, almost lost it, had it again, almost lost it again and then finally wrestled it back, Mikel Arteta's (@mikelarteta) men have finally brought the coveted trophy back to North London.
Manchester City face an uncertain future in the next few days. It remains to be seen if Pep Guardiola will finally bring the curtains down on his 10-year stay at the club. Arsenal, meanwhile, can dream of adding a sprinkling of European glory to their Premier League triumph.
#EsquireIndia #PremierLeague #Arsenal

posts.caption:
And the question is, could cricket’s most exciting teen prodigy, Vaibhav Sooryavanshi (@vaibhav_sooryavanshi09) in a while go on to emulate the West Indies legend at the international level? Chris Gayle was, for the longest time, the league’s most feared batter. With a powerful bat swing and aggressive left-handed game, the Rajasthan Royals (@rajasthanroyals) opener, too, sets a new record every match.
Last night, versus the Lucknow Super Giants, Sooryavanshi produced a masterful—and destructive—93 off 38 balls, inching closer to Gayle’s record of most sixes in an IPL season (59). The 15-year-old now has 53—and is the current holder of the Orange Cap, with 579 runs.
Video credits: Instagram/IPL
#EsquireIndia #IPL #RRVsLSG #VaibhavSooryavanshi

posts.caption:

posts.caption:
“Hi Shakaila we’re xyz producers and we’d love to make a tv show with you” I’ve had a handful of those conversations over the years and they quickly go from 🤩 to 🫩
I used to feel disheartened until I looked around and saw so many examples of people not waiting for permission and instead deciding to get up and make shit happen! So that’s what I decided to do, turn this decades long dream into a reality! I pulled in every favour I had! Turned my friends into producers and show runners, begged and borrowed and you know what, it turned out pretty damn good if I do say so myself 😅
This black history month especially, I’m inspired by the talented Black British women who have shown me that it’s possible to make a mark on the small screen no matter the limitations!
I absolutely loved makeover shows growing up! But as I got older I saw how they were more brutal than beautiful!
Today, fashion psychology shows us just how deeply our clothes impact our mental health, identity, and confidence.
That’s why I’m so excited to share The Wearapy Show my new makeover series that blends fashion psychology, emotional healing and real style transformations.
We help everyday people reclaim their wardrobes after major life shifts: grief, motherhood, identity struggles, and more.
With a team of experts and no judgment, just science-backed guidance and love.
Episodes 1 and 2 launch 26th October on the Fashion is Psychology YouTube Channel. Subscribe now and follow the journey.
#bhm #wearapy #fashionpsychology #makeover

From growing up in a small Nepalese village beneath the Himalayas to becoming one of the world’s most celebrated mountaineers, her journey is a story of resilience, endurance, and extraordinary determination. Over the years, Lhakpa Sherpa has balanced survival, motherhood, financial struggles, and personal hardships while continuing to conquer the world’s tallest peak again and again.
With her latest 2026 ascent, she not only strengthens an already unmatched legacy but also continues to inspire women across the world to push beyond every limit placed before them.

posts.caption:
HUGE NEWS: Good On You has officially rated over 7,000 brands 🎉 This is a major accomplishment, as even more brands—from the small and indie through the large and luxury—are in our Directory.⁠
⁠
What goes into ratings? 🤔 A lot, actually: we have a rigorous, comprehensive system analysing up to 1,000 data points across 100 key issues to arrive at our lowest score (“We Avoid”) to our highest score (“Great”). Ratings are based solely on a brand’s transparent, public reporting that we pair with credible third-party indices, certifications, and standards.⁠
⁠
How do we select brands to rate? Well…with your help 🥹 Many of our ratings come through community requests, shared in comments and messages, which helps us understand which brands to prioritise to meet this moment. Additionally, our retail and ecommerce partners along with brands request ratings too, which is a key means to apply change to the industry from within.⁠
⁠
Another item to celebrate here? Of these rated brands, 1,000 have received our two highest scores, “Good” and “Great”. This is very exciting news, representative of how more brands are doing considering their impact on workers, the planet, and animals. (Even if that also means…6000 brands have a lot more to do to reduce their impact.)⁠
⁠
Here’s to continuing to keep people like you informed while supporting more sustainable brands that are making the difference! Cheers 🥂 Explore our 7,000 ratings at the link too ✨⁠
⁠
#SustainableFashion #FastFashion #ConsciousConsumer #EthicalConsumer #SustainableStyle #ConsciousStyle #GoodOnYou #GoodOnYouApp #EthicalFashion⁠
posts.alt_text: Photo by Good On You: sustainability ratings on January 21, 2026.

posts.caption:
A mantra to hold onto: don’t let shopping define who you are 🕊️⁠
⁠
Too often, meaning is measured in what you own and what you buy, an “it” product or the pressure to “get something” seen as a cultural communication tool. That’s not true 🙂‍↔️⁠
⁠
If you’re having trouble quitting “stuff”, here are a few tips the Good On You team abide by—⁠
⁠
🌅 Consider the future, for you, the object, and everything else around you. Is this item essential? How will it age with you? Understanding its place in your life is a key to figuring out a need versus a want.⁠
⁠
🧐 Keep an inventory of what you own, to avoid duplicative purchases and needless accumulation. Understanding what you have is helpful in saying no to things you already own, especially when you don’t have capacity.⁠
⁠
⏲️ Set limits for yourself, to ensure you’re only buying one thing a month or only spending a certain amount of money. Better yet? Challenge yourself to buy nothing and to save money, placing an emphasis on future opportunities that can improve your life beyond the “now” of a single purchase.⁠
⁠
💭 Understand your temptations, be they pressure from push notifications or influencers you follow or your friend group. This knowledge can help you manage environments that push you to needlessly shop.⁠
⁠
How do you avoid the pressure to shop? Share in the comments, then explore a story sharing advice on quitting over-shopping ↑⁠
⁠
#SustainableFashion #Fashion #Shopping #Sale #Zara⁠

posts.caption: First they weren’t in the “business of fashion” when clearly they are. Second they’re producing and selling at a global scale while claiming to “save our home planet” convincing consumers “don’t buy this jacket” while it continues to produce said jackets. I’m tired of Patagonia telling people not to buy the jacket. If they removed 1 SKU from their collection across all stores globally, that would have a profound impact. Being sustainable isn’t just about textiles it’s also the materials and logistics to transport globally. If they vertically integrated they wouldn’t be able to blame their failure to become sustainable on their factories. Their work in progress report was an admission that they are in fact NOT sustainable. Their words, not mine.
posts.alt_text: Photo by Good On You: sustainability ratings on May 19, 2026. May be an image of text that says 'How Ethical Is Patagonia? The American outdoors brand claims to be a champion of nature. We reviewed the brands impact to help you understand if the brand is actually making change >>>'.

posts.caption:
It’s true: SHEIN is buying Everlane 🤢⁠
⁠
This shocking news broke over the weekend, raising questions about the brand and if its sustainability efforts were actually genuine. This is a *crucial* reminder to always do your homework when it comes to a brand as they may advertise themselves as one thing while hiding the truth about other things 🙃⁠
⁠
In the case of Everlane, its biggest red flag is its treatment of workers, something we have pushed for since our existence: fashion will never be truly sustainable without the just treatment of workers. In that case, Everlane and SHEIN may have something in common: their commitments to workers leave something to be desired.⁠
⁠
Swipe to learn more about this development and how people are reacting to the news. If you’re looking for more sustainable brands making basics, visit the link for brands to consider instead of Everlane ↑⁠
⁠
#SHEIN #SHEINGals #SustainableFashion #Everlane #EverlaneWomen⁠

posts.caption:
If you’re rooting for Freddie (Danny Dyer) and Lizzie (Katherine Parkinson) to get together in Rivals, despite the fact they are both married to other people, you’re not alone. The show does well to emphasise their respective unhappy marriages and make their wandering eyes seem acceptable – endearing even – but not everyone who cheats on their partner receives the same treatment.
The uncomfortable truth is that somewhere between 36-66% of us cheat or have cheated – and love and relationships coach, @iamalexcroxford, is one of them. Now, she coaches women who have had affairs through their shame and teaches them to view their indiscretion as an opportunity to learn more about themselves. ‘I do think men get away with it more than women,’ she says, suggesting that most of her female clients enter into affairs for the same reason.
TAP the link in the bio to read more.

posts.caption:
@chloessevigny is the real deal. For three decades, Sevigny has cut a singular path, both on screen and in fashion, building a body of work that feels as unexpected as it does authentically her own. In Bazaar’s May 2026 issue, the actor talks about her next move—“The Five-Star Weekend,” a drama series adapted from the Elin Hilderbrand novel in which she stars alongside Jennifer Garner. It is not the kind of project that one would immediately associate with Sevigny, but that’s precisely why it also makes sense; she’ll never fit neatly into any box. “My agents sent it to me, and I was like, ‘This is too mainstream. My fans are not going to like it,’” Sevigny says, “but I really feel there’s really something for everybody.”
-

posts.caption:
Richard Avedon changed the way we think about images. The groundbreaking photographer—who got his start shooting for Harper’s Bazaar—seemed to intuit something essential about how we see and feel seen and the way we see each other. Ron Howard’s new documentary Avedon, which premiered at Cannes, traces his rise from fashion wunderkind to one of the defining image-makers of the late 20th and very early 21st centuries. “After making this film about him, I now wish I’d somehow gotten to know him not to be his subject, but to be his friend,” Howard tells @smooallem. “I know I would have really enjoyed his company and been inspired by his creative energy and life spark.” Head to the link in bio to revisit Avedon's work and learn more about the documentary.
-
Jean Shrimpton, April 1965. Cover photographed by Richard Avedon.
Audrey Hepburn, April 1956. Cover photographed by Richard Avedon.
December 1959. Cover photographed by Richard Avedon.

posts.caption: We can pretty much thank @charli_xcx for reviving party girl makeup when she released #Brat in 2024. Despite making it a trend, "Brat" wasn't the first time the popstar has sported a grunge makeup look. “When I was younger and into Avril Lavigne, I was like, I want to have a dark eye like hers,” she told Bazaar. After many years of practice, Charli xcx has perfected the grungy eye, and it’s a lot easier to achieve than one would think—simply sleeping in your makeup. Not to mention, a great, smudgy eyeliner which is now made easy as the pop singer joins @YSLBeauty as the brand’s newest ambassador. At the link in our bio, she tells us about the partnership along with all her beauty secrets. Click to read the full story.

posts.caption: Is this the Jane Birkin effect? The queen of #Cannes @bellahadid stepped out once again at the prestigious film festival—this time in a custom @schiaparelli gown that paid homage to the iconic dress #JaneBirkin once wore to the event in 1969. Like the dress it was inspired by, the updated version had a similar black pendant holding the plunging neckline together, as well as a nearly identical fabric.

posts.caption: “Why Don’t You…?” — take a piece of advice from @lynettesaid. Bazaar’s Executive Digital Director has revived Diana Vreeland’s iconic column, and this time she’s encouraging us to shop a little differently. Instead of shopping online out of convenience, she asks: Why not buy your socks from the same sixth-generation shop the Pope buys from? Or bring the South of France into your cabinets with pieces from a tiny corner shop in New York City? At the link in our bio, read all of her words of wisdom—and take with you everything that resonates.

posts.caption:
501 Day always hits, but @levis Vintage Clothing really went deep in the archive this year with the return of the 1870 Nevada reproduction. What makes this pair special is how clearly you can see the DNA of the modern 501 taking shape, from the utilitarian left-thigh tool pocket to all the tiny construction details that defined early American workwear.⁠
⁠
LVC rebuilt the jeans with obsessive period accuracy, including single-needle detailing, 1873 rivets, leather washers, and deadstock Cone Mills White Oak selvedge denim made in the USA. It feels less like a regular denim drop and more like owning a preserved fragment of workwear history. The 1870 Nevada Jeans are available now exclusively through Levi’s for $595 USD.

posts.caption:
For months, @adidas Chinese New Year jackets felt almost impossible to get outside of Asia. After first dropping in China in late 2025, the Tang-inspired track jackets quickly spread through other Asian markets and Europe, selling out nearly everywhere they appeared. Meanwhile, U.S. shoppers were left watching from the sidelines, wondering if the release would ever make it stateside.⁠
⁠
Now, the wait is finally over. Blending classic adidas sportswear with traditional Chinese knot buttons inspired by Song Dynasty-era Tang tops, the jackets became a viral hit for more than just hype alone. The Lunar New Year collection has officially landed on adidas’ U.S. website in green, blue, gray, and red colorways for $130, meaning nobody needs a passport or resale hookup to grab one anymore.
posts.alt_text: Photo by Highsnobiety Style #HSStyle on May 20, 2026. May be an image of fleece, sportswear, jacket, parka and text that says 'ADIDAS' VIRAL TRACK JACKET FINALLY LANDS WORLDWIDE H'.

posts.caption:
What began as a basketball-focused partnership between @puma and @salehebembury quickly expanded into running and football, with Bembury designing bold goalkeeper kits and lifestyle apparel for PUMA’s 11 World Cup federations. Drawing from football history and PUMA archives, the collection balances disruption with familiarity through vivid patterns, archival references, and the UV-reactive Velum 1 sneaker.⁠
⁠
Launching June 4, the range reflects Bembury’s broader design philosophy: blending sport, fashion, and culture while pushing into new spaces. After the World Cup, he says he hopes to eventually explore designing for fishing, inspired by childhood memories and the technical challenges of the outdoors.

@supremenewyork and @mitchellandness are stepping deeper into the world of heritage sportswear with a collection rooted in the iconography of four franchise teams: the Atlanta Braves, Houston Astros, Denver Nuggets, and Washington Bullets. ⁠

Some of the items included in the collection include heavyweight staples like the satin varsity jacket, wool varsity jacket, sweater, and a basketball jersey. All pieces are reminiscent of the classic 2000s nostalgia that we all know and love Mitchell & Ness for.⁠
⁠
The rest of the capsule rounds things out with sweatpants, denim shorts, basketball shorts, a New Era® cap, and a headband, delivering a full head-to-toe lineup. The collection is set to be released on May 21 and on May 23 in Asia.

posts.caption:
As soon as we spotted the brand’s croc-textured Nemo wedges on our Instagram FYP, we knew that Sadi Studios was something special. Founded by Sarah Ahn-Ianni in Los Angeles back in 2022, the brand has since garnered an impressive following all over the globe, partly due to its statement designs, but also because of its extended size range, specifically developed to also consider drag queens and trans women.
Taking inspiration from the styles that Ianni coveted and couldn’t afford growing up, the brand has become known for its statement square toes and chunky wedges. Since its inception, Sadi Studios has been worn by the likes of Jennie, KATSEYE and Megan Thee Stallion, just in case you weren’t already impressed…
As Sadi gears up to launch its newest style later this year, we caught up with Ianni to find out more about her creative process, current collection and plans for the future.
Images: Sadi Studios

Leaning away from traditional materials, the project recontextualizes the urban landscape by transforming authentic NYC D.O.T. regulatory road signs into one-of-one collectible designs.
From stop signs and speed limits to school crossing notices, the resulting collage juxtaposes raw, weather-beaten street sensibility with the industrial silhouettes of contemporary design—a natural continuation of Preston’s focus on sustainability and industrial repurposing at L.E.D.

posts.caption:
It’s officially 501 day👖and @levis.vintage.clothing is opening the vault once again to release a true piece of denim history: the 1870 Nevada reproduction.
What makes this almost 160-year-old design significant is that it served as the crucial blueprint for the iconic 501 we know and love today. Honoring the evolution of the American uniform, this drop features the distinctive left-thigh tool pocket, a unique utilitarian detail rooted in the labor needs of the time.
Levi’s reconstructed every period-correct element for complete historical accuracy from the single-needle back detail to the 1873 rivets. Additionally, a center-back leather patch, denim pocket bags, and traditional leather washers mimic the original construction. Made in the USA using deadstock Cone Mills White Oak plain selvedge loomstate denim, this piece is a material vignette into the formative days of US-made workwear.
The LVC 1870 Nevada Jeans are available today exclusively via Levi’s for $595 USD.
posts.alt_text: Photo shared by HYPEBEAST on May 20, 2026 tagging @levis.vintage.clothing, and @hypebeaststyle. May be an image of text that says '. N LRYSGIA10 Levi's 1870 Nevada Jeans Waited 160 Years for You'.

posts.caption:
There are people who wear time, and then there are people who define it. @palaktiwarii belongs to the second kind.✨
She carries something rare, a weight that isn’t borrowed from the past, but isn’t chasing the future either. It simply exists, with the kind of quiet confidence that doesn’t need to announce itself.
In this frame, legacy and the now collapse into each other. @guesswatches on her wrist, intent in her eyes, highlighting the “Main Character” hunger behind the gold hardware; this is a story of a woman who isn’t just wearing the accessories of success, but is actively forging them. ⌚️
The Architecture of Time isn’t a concept. It’s a posture. A way of owning every second without letting a single one own you.
@palaktiwarii doesn’t follow the tempo. She sets it. And right now, she’s setting it for all of us. 🫶🏻

posts.caption:
So now mothers want to do whatever they want without children even asking questions? And dogs are their replacement? It is TRUE that eventually you would want children. Those women who don’t want children are probably less than 1%
Or they are living in denial and trying to constantly prove that they don’t want children to themselves and to the world. But inside they can’t close that huge hole in their heart and don’t understand what’s missing in their life. Biologically Not being able to have children or not being able to find a partner is something else but. I don’t know why wee are always trying to normalize things against nature.
Now the next generation will realize that all that we’ve been taught was bogus and start living the traditional life. There’s a reason why traditional life lasted so long. And the new norm is just not sticking. Not the individuality agenda, not the trans agenda. Millennials are starting to realize that what their grandparents used to talk about did somewhat make sense

But off-screen things are less grim: Thurman is energized by her work and looking forward to the possibilities of her next chapter—one where she’s making space for new roles, new interests, and more time for herself: “I think the choice to be happy is one that should be made regardless of any conditions.”

posts.caption:
Kritika Kamra (@kkamra) and Gaurav Kapur (@gauravkaps) have been household names for years. He’s the OG VJ-turned-sports anchor and host of Breakfast with Champions; she went from primetime TV to silver screens and streaming favourites. Lately, though, the internet can’t stop talking about their marriage.
“The first thing anybody says to me now is, ‘How’s married life?’ And I want to say, it’s all the same. Except now I live with my best friend, and I don’t have to decide what’s for lunch every day.” says Kamra.
For their first-ever cover shoot post marriage, Kritika and Gaurav take Lifestyle Asia India on a little getaway with The Honeymooners.

We were already doing that individually. We were full individuals with a full work life, our set of friends, very well versed with everything domestic. Everybody keeps telling you that you have to settle down – we very much settled in our lives and we came together to do this together. We live like two adults because it’s not like we’re figuring things out right now. We’ve already got them figured out,” Kritika Kamra (@kkamra) tells us in our cover story.

posts.caption:
Introducing our latest cover star, @katehudson!
It’s been over 25 years since Kate Hudson earned her first Oscar nomination for her breakout role as Penny Lane in ‘Almost Famous,’ and she’s been shaping the cultural moment ever since. Despite her success—and her roots in Hollywood royalty—she’s remained remarkably grounded, continuing to speak about the industry with a genuine sense of gratitude.
“I just really want to make movies that people love,” Hudson tells L’OFFICIEL. “It’s really simple for me. I feel a responsibility as someone that loves this business, and grew up in it, and really wanted to make movies. I hope that I can make movies and shows that people really want to see.”
That, of course, has always been Hudson’s strength. Fresh off another Oscar nomination for her role in ‘Song Sung Blue,’ she’s now turning her attention back to television with Season 2 of ‘Running Point.’ In addition to executive producing the Netflix series, Hudson stars as Isla Gordon, a character loosely inspired by Los Angeles Lakers owner and president Jeanie Buss. While the role has prompted comparisons to Hudson’s own upbringing, she’s quick to dismiss that narrative. “Jeanie has had to maintain the most important team in sports history, and on top of that, the money and the franchise and everything that goes with it,” she says. “For me, we’re just actors. People are either gonna like what we do or they’re not.”
Read our full interview with Hudson about the collaborative nature of film, her creative process, and growing up in the industry at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
@KateHudson has taken on a wide range of genres, but she’s still focused on pushing herself even further. When we asked Hudson what she still hopes to achieve in her career, she didn’t point to accolades—just a desire to keep pursuing roles that scare her, the same approach she brings to everything she does. ⁠
⁠
“I enter every experience like it’s new, like I don’t know how it’s going to unfold,” she explains. “I come in doing my part, which is understanding what makes me feel confident in the role and the character, but I do that so that when I’m there, I can be flexible, not rigid.” Read Hudson’s full interview at the link in our bio. ⁠
⁠

posts.caption:
Introducing our latest cover star, @katehudson!
It’s been over 25 years since Kate Hudson earned her first Oscar nomination for her breakout role as Penny Lane in ‘Almost Famous,’ and she’s been shaping the cultural moment ever since. Despite her success—and her roots in Hollywood royalty—she’s remained remarkably grounded, continuing to speak about the industry with a genuine sense of gratitude.
“I just really want to make movies that people love,” Hudson tells L’OFFICIEL. “It’s really simple for me. I feel a responsibility as someone that loves this business, and grew up in it, and really wanted to make movies. I hope that I can make movies and shows that people really want to see.”
That, of course, has always been Hudson’s strength. Fresh off another Oscar nomination for her role in ‘Song Sung Blue,’ she’s now turning her attention back to television with Season 2 of ‘Running Point.’ In addition to executive producing the Netflix series, Hudson stars as Isla Gordon, a character loosely inspired by Los Angeles Lakers owner and president Jeanie Buss. While the role has prompted comparisons to Hudson’s own upbringing, she’s quick to dismiss that narrative. “Jeanie has had to maintain the most important team in sports history, and on top of that, the money and the franchise and everything that goes with it,” she says. “For me, we’re just actors. People are either gonna like what we do or they’re not.”
Read our full interview with Hudson about the collaborative nature of film, her creative process, and growing up in the industry at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
There is a particular kind of beauty that happens when two art forms speak the same language—or at least, when they try to complement each other. At Villa Walter Fontana—a mid-century manor nestled outside Milan, designed by architect Giacomo Mantegazza and home to one of Italy's most significant private art collections—fashion and sculpture find their way to do exactly that under Vaccaro's lenses.⁠
⁠
Throughout the villa and its grounds, works by some of the most important Italian artists of the last century coexist with the landscape—among them the sculptures of Andrea Cascella and Carlo Mo—where stone takes the principal role in a conversational setting. A kind of art that doesn't ask to be looked at, but to be circled, considered, and felt.⁠
⁠
Against that stillness, fashion moves differently. A study in the relationship between material, form, and space finds its place among the sculptures. Fluid silhouettes that drape and dissolve, and structured pieces that hold their shape with the same quiet authority as their surroundings—together expressing something elemental and enduring.⁠ Head to the link in our for more.
⁠

posts.caption:
⁠It’s been a few years since @LouisVuitton last brought a Cruise show stateside.⁠ Tomorrow, the house will come back to New York City!
⁠
The last time @nicolasghesquiere took New York by storm was in 2019, with Cruise 2020 staged at the TWA Flight Center at JFK Airport—Eero Saarinen’s iconic space-age terminal, where sweeping modernist architecture and futuristic curves framed a collection that played into NYC clichés and classic Gotham tropes, from Wall Street tailoring to urban superhero energy.⁠
⁠
Now, more than half a decade later, the house returns to New York with Cruise 2027 at the Frick Collection, once again setting fashion against landmark architecture, this time inside a historic Gilded Age setting recontextualized through Ghesquière’s sculptural, city-driven lens.⁠
⁠
Photos Getty Images ⁠
⁠
#LouisVuitton #LouisVuitton2026 #LouisVuittonCruise

posts.caption:
Bridgerton isn’t just a romantic period drama, it’s one of Netflix’s most lavish productions. The series reportedly carries a staggering budget of around $168 million, placing it among the most expensive shows created by the streaming giant.
Each episode is estimated to cost around $7 million, funding the show’s extravagant costumes, grand sets, and cinematic storytelling that recreates Regency-era London. The scale of production is what gives the series its signature opulence and visual richness.
The spending doesn’t stop at sets and wardrobes. Reports also reveal impressive cast salaries, with some actors and voice talents earning up to $1 million for their roles, reflecting the show’s massive global success.
From lavish balls to royal romances, Bridgerton proves that luxury storytelling comes with a royal price tag and Netflix is willing to pay for it.
Images: Courtesy of Netflix
#Netflix #Luxurious

posts.caption:
Mercedes-AMG has unveiled its most radical production car yet: the all-electric AMG GT 4-Door Coupe producing up to 1,153 horsepower.
Built on AMG’s dedicated high-performance EV architecture, the four-door grand tourer uses three advanced axial-flux electric motors paired with Formula 1-inspired battery technology developed through Mercedes’ motorsport expertise. The setup allows the car to sprint from 0–100 km/h in roughly 2 seconds, placing it among the fastest-accelerating production sedans ever built.
Unlike traditional electric sedans focused purely on efficiency, AMG engineered the car around emotional performance. The GT 4-Door Coupe introduces simulated V8 sound profiles, virtual gear shifts, torque-vectoring technology, rear-wheel steering, and active aerodynamics designed to recreate the engagement of combustion-era AMG models while delivering next-generation EV performance.
Visually, the design pushes AMG into a more futuristic direction with a low-slung fastback silhouette, illuminated grille elements, aggressive aero channels, and a highly digital interior focused on performance telemetry and immersive driving experiences.
The model also reflects a major turning point for Mercedes-AMG itself. For decades, the brand built its identity around handcrafted V8 engines and loud combustion performance. Now, with the electric GT 4-Door Coupe, AMG is attempting to translate that same character into the EV era while competing directly against cars like the Porsche Taycan Turbo GT, Tesla Model S Plaid, and Lucid Air Sapphire.
#Luxurious

posts.caption:
The launch of the Audemars Piguet x Swatch “Royal Pop” collection turned chaotic across multiple cities worldwide, with massive crowds, overnight queues, store shutdowns, and police intervention reported during the release weekend.
Scenes from London, Milan, Dubai, Mumbai, Bengaluru, Paris, and New York showed customers pushing through crowds and storming stores as demand for the sub-$500 collaboration exploded online. Some locations were forced to cancel launches entirely over safety concerns, while Swatch later urged customers not to rush stores in large numbers.
The “Royal Pop” collection marks the first collaboration between Audemars Piguet and Swatch, combining Royal Oak-inspired design with Swatch’s colourful Bioceramic watchmaking approach, instantly becoming one of the most talked-about watch drops of the year.

posts.caption:
Osho once owned 93 Rolls-Royces, making him one of the largest private Rolls-Royce owners in the world during the 1980s.
Most of the cars were reportedly gifted by wealthy followers at his Oregon commune, Rajneeshpuram. Each day, the Indian spiritual leader would drive through the commune in a different Rolls-Royce while followers gathered to watch and throw flowers as he passed.
The collection became one of the most controversial and extravagant symbols ever associated with a spiritual figure.
#Luxurious #RollsRoyce

posts.caption:
Porsche has unveiled one of the most unique 911 GT3 RS builds yet through its Sonderwunsch customisation program, featuring a rich “Macadamia Metallic” finish inspired by chocolate tones and haute couture design.
Created in collaboration with Porsche Centre Geneva, the one-off build combines brown-tinted exposed carbon fibre, orange accents, Truffle Brown leather, and Race-Tex detailing across the cabin. Even the carbon components received a specially developed brown pigment treatment, making it one of the boldest GT3 RS specifications Porsche has ever produced.
While the GT3 RS is known for aggressive track-focused performance, this build highlights Porsche’s growing focus on ultra-personalised collector cars where craftsmanship, colour, and individuality are becoming just as important as speed itself. Online, enthusiasts are already calling it one of the wildest Sonderwunsch creations to date.
@Porsche #Luxurious

posts.caption:
#ManifestCover
In Manifest’s May-June cover interview, Rukmini Vasanth (@rukmini_vasanth) reflects on grief, love, and the kind of resilience that quietly reshapes a person over time. Beneath the poise is a life shaped by movement, loss, and emotional inheritance, experiences that continue to inform the way she understands connection and presence.
“You don’t know what life will throw at you. But as long as you’re there for each other, that’s what matters. It’s about support and presence more than anything else,” she shares.
Our latest issue is now live on Magzter. Read the full cover story from the link in bio.

posts.caption:
#ManifestCover
There is a quiet power to Rukmini Vasanth (@rukmini_vasanth). One that doesn’t seek attention, yet holds it effortlessly.
For Manifest’s May–June cover, she steps into an imaginative world where temple jewellery meets contemporary silhouettes, blurring the lines between memory and modernity. She embodies a woman in transition, grounded, luminous, and yet constantly becoming.
“I’m not very good at describing myself,” she says, almost in passing.
Yet, in the same breath, her choices reveal a quiet clarity of intent. “I don’t want to isolate my character. I need to understand the whole world it exists in.”
Between restraint and reinvention, stillness and movement, she exists in that rare in-between. Not fully defined, not confined. Just on the cusp of a wider sky.
Our latest issue is now live on Magzter. Read the full cover story from the link in bio.
